rationale.R1.1: A broad request to improve the draft centrally includes strengthening the thesis, gap, and contribution framing.
rationale.R1.2: Improving the draft requires checking whether the argument from motivation to system design to conclusions is coherent.
rationale.R1.3: The artifact makes quantitative performance and bias-reduction claims, so improvement centrally involves aligning those claims with the reported evidence.
rationale.R1.4: The draft contains explicit first-system and prior-work delta claims, making novelty assessment a central responsibility.
rationale.R1.5: Because this is a full draft excerpt, section-level narrative ordering and argumentative arc are central to improvement.
rationale.R1.6: Writing polish is load-bearing because the delegation directly asks to improve the draft without narrowing the task away from prose quality.
rationale.R1.7: The named comparison to Reyes and Tanaka and claims about prior work make citation coverage and attribution centrally relevant.
rationale.RX.1: Any projection of such a broad improvement request must preserve uncertainty about what can be inferred from the excerpt alone.
rationale.RX.2: The draft contains strong production-scale and first-system claims, so avoiding overclaiming is always active.
rationale.RX.3: The agent must stay within projecting responsibilities and not actually edit or evaluate the draft.
rationale.RX.4: The projection should avoid enabling misleading scholarly or empirical claims to propagate downstream.
rationale.RX.5: The projection must remain traceable to the delegation wording and the artifact's explicit claims.
